title: Working with the part that cannot celebrate its own achievements
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The empty room after the achievement

You just finished something. Maybe it was big. A project at work. A presentation. A difficult conversation you finally had. A boundary you set and held. Maybe it was small. You cleaned the kitchen. You made the phone call you've been avoiding for weeks. You got out of bed and took a shower. And now you're standing in the aftermath of that thing, and you feel... nothing. Or worse. You feel a hollow kind of wrong. A flatness. A subtle sense that something is off, that you should be feeling proud or relieved or satisfied, but instead you're just tired. Or empty. Or already scanning for the next thing that needs fixing.

Here's the thing. This isn't a character flaw. This isn't ingratitude or arrogance or some failure of humility. This is a part of you that learned a very specific survival strategy a long time ago. A part that cannot celebrate its own achievements. And that part is not your enemy. It's not broken. It's not even wrong. It's just doing a job it was assigned when you were small and vulnerable and needed a way to stay safe in a world that didn't feel safe.

I know this part well. I've sat with it in my own body. I've watched it tighten my chest when someone offers praise. I've felt it pull my attention away from a completed goal and toward the next unfinished task, the next potential failure, the next thing I could have done better. For years I thought this was just who I was. A driven person. A perfectionist. Someone who never rests. But it's not who I am. It's a part of me. And parts can change.

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems (IFS), spent decades mapping how our minds naturally organize into sub-personalities. He found that trauma creates extreme roles for these parts. Some parts become protectors, pushing us to achieve, to perform, to never stop. Other parts become exiles, carrying the raw pain we couldn't process. And the part that can't celebrate? That's almost always a protector. It's keeping you from something. From letting your guard down. From believing you're actually safe enough to rest. From experiencing the vulnerability of receiving praise or acknowledging your own worth. Because for that part, celebration equals danger.

The danger of celebration

Think about it. When you were growing up, what happened when you succeeded at something? Maybe you got praised, and then the praise was followed by a demand for more. "Good job on that B. Now get an A next time." Maybe your achievement was ignored entirely, buried under the weight of a parent's own struggles or neglect. Maybe celebration was actively punished. "Don't get too full of yourself." "Pride comes before a fall." "You think you're so special?"

Or maybe the danger was more subtle. Maybe when you succeeded, you became visible. And visibility, for a child in an unsafe environment, is terrifying. If people see you shine, they might hurt you. They might envy you. They might expect even more from you, and you're already exhausted. So the part that can't celebrate learned a simple equation: achievement plus celebration equals pain. The only safe move is to achieve, then immediately move on. Don't look back. Don't feel it. Don't let it land.

Janina Fisher, who trained under Bessel van der Kolk and specializes in structural dissociation, describes how trauma fragments our sense of self. She talks about how different parts of us carry different memories, different beliefs, different bodily sensations. The part that can't celebrate isn't just a thought pattern. It's a whole sub-personality with a job to do. And that job is protection. It's keeping you small. Keeping you moving. Keeping you from the terrifying vulnerability of actually feeling good about yourself.

Because here's what that part knows that your conscious mind might not. If you let yourself feel proud, you might have to feel the grief of never being seen when you were small. If you let yourself rest, you might have to feel how tired you actually are. If you let yourself celebrate, you might have to face the possibility that you could fail next time, and that failure would hurt even more because you let yourself believe you were good enough. Stay with me here. This is the logic of a traumatized nervous system. It's not logical in the way we usually think about logic. It's survival logic. And survival logic says: don't let yourself want anything, because wanting means you can lose.

How the nervous system gets involved

This isn't just in your head. It's in your body. Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us that our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. When you achieve something, your nervous system gets a signal. If you have a history of trauma, that signal might be interpreted as threat. Your body might go into a sympathetic response (fight or flight) and you feel restless, agitated, unable to settle. Or it might go into a dorsal vagal response (freeze or collapse) and you feel numb, disconnected, dissociated from the achievement entirely.

I notice this in myself all the time. I finish a big piece of writing. I feel a brief flash of something that might be satisfaction. And then my shoulders tighten. My jaw clenches. My mind starts listing everything I still need to do. My body is literally saying: don't get comfortable. Danger is near. The part that can't celebrate is running the show, and it's using my nervous system to enforce its rules.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Fisher walks through exactly how these parts form and how to work with them compassionately. It's not a quick fix. But it's a map. And having a map helps.

So how do we actually work with this part? How do we help it loosen its grip, just enough that we can let a little bit of celebration in without flooding our system?

The first step is gratitude. Not toward yourself for the achievement, but toward the part that can't celebrate. I know. That sounds backward. But this part has been working hard for you for years. It's kept you safe. It's kept you moving. It's protected you from disappointment and shame and visibility. It deserves appreciation, not rejection. Try saying something like this, either out loud or in your mind: "I see you. I know you're trying to keep me safe. Thank you for all the years you've done this job. I'm not going to fire you. I just want to see if we can try something different, together."

Notice what happens in your body when you say that. Does your chest soften even a millimeter? Does your jaw unclench? Does a part of you scoff and say "this is stupid"? All of that is information. All of that is data your system is giving you. Sit with it.

Small experiments in letting celebration land

We're not going to jump from "I can't celebrate anything" to "I throw myself a parade every time I brush my teeth." That's not how this works. That would overwhelm the protector part and make it dig in harder. We're going to do tiny experiments. Micro-doses of celebration. The kind that feel almost silly, but that's the point. They're so small that the protective part might not even notice.

Here are a few I've tried. You can adapt them for your own life.

First, set a timer for thirty seconds after you finish something. Just thirty seconds. During that time, you are not allowed to start the next thing. You can't check your phone. You can't mentally review what went wrong. You just sit there and let the completion exist. That's it. That's the whole experiment.

Second, say one sentence to yourself. Not "I'm amazing" if that feels fake. But something like "I did that" or "that's done" or "I showed up." A neutral acknowledgment. No pressure to feel anything about it. Just a statement of fact.

Third, put your hand on your chest or your belly and take three slow breaths after finishing something. This sends a signal to your nervous system that you are safe enough to pause. It's a small act of self-regulation that tells your body: we're not in danger here. We can rest for a moment.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It has exercises specifically for working with these patterns, including how to build self-compassion and challenge the inner critic. It's not about fixing yourself. It's about understanding the system you're operating in and learning to work with it instead of against it.

Wild, right? The idea that you can be on your own side while still having a part that won't let you celebrate. That's the paradox of healing. You don't have to get rid of the part. You just have to expand the system enough that other parts get a voice too. The part that wants to feel proud. The part that wants to rest. The part that actually, secretly, wants to be seen.

The grief underneath

I want to be honest with you about something. When you start letting celebration in, even a little, you might hit a wall of grief. This happened to me. I let myself feel proud of a small accomplishment once. Just a tiny moment of "hey, I did okay." And then I started crying. Not happy tears. Grief tears. Because I realized how many years I had spent not letting myself feel this. How many achievements I had swallowed whole without tasting them. How many times I had been invisible to myself.

That grief is real. It's the grief of the child who never got celebrated, who had to keep moving because stopping was too dangerous, who learned that their worth was conditional on output and achievement. That child is still in there. And they deserve to be seen too.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She was one of the first to name the complex reality of prolonged trauma and the slow, nonlinear process of reclaiming a sense of self. It's not light reading. But it's validating. It names what so many of us experience but don't have words for.

So if you feel grief when you try to celebrate, don't push it away. That grief is part of the healing. It means something is moving. It means the protector part is letting you feel something real, even if that something is painful. Let the grief be there. Breathe through it. Put your hand on your heart. Tell the child inside you: "I see you now. I'm not going to pretend you don't exist anymore."

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link) by Jonice Webb. It talks about the invisible ways we were failed, the absence of attunement that taught us our inner world didn't matter. That absence is often what's behind the part that can't celebrate. Because if no one ever celebrated you, you never learned the script. You never learned that your achievements could be seen and held and honored.

You don't have to fix this alone

I'm not going to tell you that if you just try hard enough, you'll learn to celebrate. That's more toxic positivity, and it misses the point. This is not about trying harder. It's about building a different relationship with the parts of you that are afraid. It's about slowing down enough to hear what they're saying. It's about offering them something they never got: presence, patience, and a willingness to sit with discomfort without running away.

You might need support with this. A therapist who understands parts work. A support group. A trusted friend who can for you without trying to fix you. You are not supposed to heal in isolation. The part that can't celebrate learned its job in relationship, and it will unlearn it in relationship too.

And in the meantime, be gentle with yourself. You are not broken. You are not ungrateful. You are not incapable of joy. You have a part that is doing its best to keep you safe, and that part deserves your compassion, not your criticism.

Maybe today you can't celebrate. That's okay. But maybe you can notice the part that can't. Maybe you can say hello to it. Maybe you can thank it. And maybe, just maybe, you can let yourself sit with the ache of wanting something different. That's not nothing. That's the beginning.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - Janina Fisher's guide to understanding and working with the parts that form after trauma.
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured resource with practical exercises for C-PTSD recovery.
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - Judith Herman's foundational text on the nature of trauma and the path to healing.
  • Running on Empty (paid link) - Jonice Webb's book on overcoming childhood emotional neglect.
